subject: ap-world-history
theme: Revolutions, 1750–1900: Enlightenment ideas and industrialization trigger political revolutions, independence movements, and calls for social reform across the Atlantic world.

core_concepts.title: The Enlightenment: Intellectual Foundations of Revolution
core_concepts.summary: The Enlightenment (c. 1680–1800) was an intellectual movement arguing that reason, rather than tradition or religion, should guide human understanding and political organization. Key thinkers: John Locke (natural rights, government by consent, right of revolution); Jean-Jacques Rousseau (social contract, popular sovereignty); Baron de Montesquieu (separation of powers); Voltaire (religious tolerance, free speech); Adam Smith (laissez-faire economics, Wealth of Nations). These ideas challenged absolutism and aristocratic privilege, providing the ideological framework for the American, French, and Latin American revolutions.

core_concepts.title: The American Revolution
core_concepts.summary: The American Revolution (1775–1783) was the first successful anticolonial revolution inspired by Enlightenment principles. Colonial elites, angered by British taxation without parliamentary representation after the costly Seven Years' War (1756–1763), declared independence in 1776. The Declaration of Independence drew directly on Locke's natural rights theory. The new US Constitution (1787) embodied Montesquieu's separation of powers and Lockean limited government. The American Revolution's significance: it demonstrated that Enlightenment principles could be translated into a functioning republic, inspiring subsequent revolutions globally.

core_concepts.title: The French Revolution and Napoleon
core_concepts.summary: The French Revolution (1789–1799) began with fiscal crisis and class conflict and escalated into radical transformation and terror. Phases: Estates-General → Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) → Constitutional Monarchy → Radical Republic (Reign of Terror, 1793–94, ~17,000 executions under Robespierre) → Directory → Napoleon's coup (1799). Napoleon Bonaparte (r. 1799–1815) spread revolutionary legal principles (Napoleonic Code — equality before law, property rights, abolished feudalism) across conquered Europe, but also reintroduced slavery in Haiti and concentrated power in himself. Napoleon's conquests inadvertently spread nationalism: conquered peoples resented French rule, stimulating resistance movements.

core_concepts.title: The Haitian Revolution
core_concepts.summary: The Haitian Revolution (1791–1804) was the only successful slave revolt in history, creating the first Black republic. Saint-Domingue (Haiti) was France's most profitable colony, producing 40% of Europe's sugar and 60% of its coffee through brutal enslaved labor. The Revolution began among free people of color (inspired by French Revolution rhetoric), escalated when enslaved people revolted under Toussaint Louverture, and concluded when Jean-Jacques Dessalines declared independence in 1804 after defeating Napoleon's expeditionary force. The Haitian Revolution terrified slaveholders across the Americas and inspired enslaved people everywhere.

core_concepts.title: Latin American Independence Movements
core_concepts.summary: Between 1808 and 1826, most of Spanish and Portuguese America achieved independence. Napoleon's invasion of Spain (1808), which installed his brother as king, triggered colonial crises of legitimacy. Independence movements were led by criollos — American-born Europeans — who resented peninsular dominance but feared lower-class and indigenous revolution (as in Mexico's Hidalgo revolt). Simón Bolívar (Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Bolivia) and José de San Martín (Argentina, Chile) led military campaigns across South America. Brazil achieved relatively peaceful independence (1822) when Prince Pedro declared it while Portugal was in political turmoil. Independence transferred power from peninsulares to criollos but did not fundamentally alter racial or economic hierarchies.

core_concepts.title: The Industrial Revolution: Causes and Consequences
core_concepts.summary: Britain's Industrial Revolution (c. 1760–1840) transformed global economic and social structures. Enabling factors: coal and iron resources, reliable property rights, capital from colonial trade, and the canal/rail transportation network. Key innovations: steam engine (James Watt, 1769), textile machinery (spinning jenny, power loom), iron production, railways. Social consequences: urbanization, factory labor replacing artisanal production, child and female labor, dangerous working conditions, new industrial working class (proletariat). The industrial revolution generated both unprecedented wealth and profound inequality — stimulating socialist critiques (Marx) and labor organizing. Britain's industrial dominance was later challenged by Germany, the US, and Japan.

key_terms.definition: 18th-century intellectual movement emphasizing reason, individual rights, and representative government; provided ideological foundations for the American, French, and Latin American revolutions.
John Locke's concept that individuals possess inherent rights (life, liberty, property) that governments cannot legitimately violate — foundational to American and French revolutionary thought.
Rousseau's theory that government legitimacy derives from the consent of the governed; if government violates the contract, the people have the right to dissolve it.
Montesquieu's principle that government authority should be divided among executive, legislative, and judicial branches to prevent tyranny — incorporated into the US Constitution.
Document drafted primarily by Thomas Jefferson declaring American independence from Britain; drew directly on Locke's natural rights theory.
Radical phase of the French Revolution (1793–1794) under the Committee of Public Safety led by Robespierre; approximately 17,000 people were executed as counterrevolutionaries.
Legal code promulgated by Napoleon (1804) establishing equality before law, property rights, and freedom of religion; spread across Napoleonic-occupied Europe, overturning feudal law.
Leader of the Haitian Revolution (1791–1803), a formerly enslaved person who defeated French, British, and Spanish forces before being captured by Napoleon and dying in French imprisonment.
Person of full European descent born in the Americas; typically resented exclusion from high colonial offices reserved for peninsulares, and led most Latin American independence movements.
Criollo military leader who liberated Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia from Spanish rule (c. 1810–1826); hoped to create a unified South American confederation.
Karl Marx's term for the industrial working class who owned no means of production and survived by selling their labor — the class he predicted would lead communist revolution.
1848 pamphlet by Marx and Engels arguing history is driven by class struggle and calling on workers to unite, overthrow capitalism, and establish communism.
Early 19th-century English artisans who smashed industrial machinery in protest against factories that destroyed their skilled trades — early example of anti-industrialization resistance.
Japanese political revolution that restored imperial rule, launched rapid modernization and industrialization, and built a Western-style military to resist colonization.

Political ideology asserting that people sharing a common language, culture, or ethnicity should form their own sovereign state; drove German and Italian unification and later anti-colonial movements.

exam_tip: Revolution comparison is the most common Unit 5 essay type. Know the Haitian Revolution's key differences from other Atlantic revolutions: it was the only successful slave revolt, created the only Black republic, and its leaders were from the enslaved class, not educated elites. For causation essays about the Industrial Revolution, prioritize arguing that Britain's industrialization was made possible by colonial wealth — cotton from India and the Americas, markets created by imperialism — connecting Unit 5 to Unit 6. Always link Enlightenment thinkers to specific revolutionary documents.
